text.title: xp-extreme-programming Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for xp-extreme-programming. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Evaluate design proposals in this priority order: (1) passes all tests, (2) reveals intention, (3) no duplication, (4) fewest elements. When in conflict, earlier takes precedence.
rule.rationale: LLMs over-engineer by defaulting to "future-proof" designs. The priority order is an explicit rejection function: any design that adds elements without satisfying a failing test violates YAGNI.

rule.statement: Commit at least once per day. All tests must pass locally before commit. Never leave the build broken. Fix failures immediately — drop everything else.
rule.rationale: A broken build blocks every team member. The XP contract is: integration is everyone's responsibility, not the last person to push.

rule.statement: Track velocity in completed stories per iteration, not lines of code. LLMs inflate LOC — velocity in LOC is meaningless when an agent writes boilerplate.
rule.rationale: Story completion maps to actual business value delivery. LOC is a proxy that breaks down entirely with AI assistance.

rule.statement: Human writes the failing test first, then shows it to Claude. Claude proposes the minimal green implementation. Human approves before Claude writes anything.
rule.rationale: "AI as pair" hype skips the disagreement function of pairing. LLMs default to compliance. The human-writes-test-first discipline forces specification before implementation and prevents Claude from generating both test and source in one shot.

rule.statement: Forbid the agent from editing both the test file and the source file in the same tool call. Test edit must be a separate commit before the implementation edit.
rule.rationale: When agent edits both simultaneously, TDD discipline collapses — the test is written to match the implementation rather than specify it.

rule.statement: "Done" means: all tests pass, no TODO/FIXME left in changed files, CHANGELOG updated for the release, no warnings introduced. The PR template carries the Done checklist; the CI gate enforces it.
rule.rationale: "Mostly done" is the team's slowest tax; codifying Done into the CI gate removes ambiguity.

rule.statement: Adopt in phases: phase 1 = substrate (tests + CI + small commits); phase 2 = flow (small releases, refactor, YAGNI); phase 3 = craft (pair, collective ownership, metaphor). Do NOT attempt phase 3 before phase 1 is green.
rule.rationale: Pair-programming before CI is mostly wasted — the pair fixes the same broken build twice.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (substrate signals available, release cadence weekly+), execute: score 12 practices → identify next phase → adopt → measure → re-score quarterly.
rule.rationale: Adoption without scoring becomes "we do agile". Scoring forces honesty.

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) regulated env mandates a different process, (b) long research cycle precludes small releases, (c) team hostile to process — escalate first, (d) adoption tried and rolled back in last 12 months — investigate why.
rule.rationale: XP requires substrate + cadence + cultural will.
